content:
AI is one of the biggest technological shifts I’ve seen in my career. It has also become remarkably easy to start with the technology instead of the problem.
Over the past couple of years, I’ve spent a lot of evenings and weekends building AI projects. Some had a practical purpose. Others were simply an excuse to explore an idea that caught my attention. I’ve always learned best by building things and more importantly, by finding where they stop working.
What surprised me wasn’t which projects succeeded and which failed. It was how often I discovered I’d started with the wrong question.

• For one project, I was convinced I needed a large language model. I didn’t. A much simpler neural network produced better results.

• Another started as a local chatbot. Before long, I wasn’t really thinking about chat anymore. The interesting questions became information retrieval, memory, and what actually makes an assistant useful in different contexts.

• Then there was a project where I spent weeks trying to improve a model before realizing the model wasn’t really the issue. I had framed the problem poorly. Once I changed the question, the solution became much clearer.
The pattern wasn’t obvious while I was building them. It only became clear when I looked back. While I wouldn’t consider those projects failures, none of them taught me what I expected, either. I kept starting with the technology. A cool new model to try. A new framework I’d just read about. A YouTube tutorial touting the latest tool.
That’s an easy trap to fall into. AI is moving so fast that every week brings another model, another framework, another benchmark, and another prediction about how everything is about to change. It’s exciting. I enjoy following it as much as anyone.
But somewhere along the way, I stopped asking, “How can I use AI here?” and started asking, “What problem am I actually trying to solve?” That may seem like a subtle difference, but in my experience, it isn’t.
Back in graduate school, I had a friend named Gabe. Every time I raised my hand in class, he’d mutter, “Making friends.” He knew exactly what was coming. I was about to test whatever assumption the professor had just introduced. Not to be difficult, but because I wanted to understand where the idea stopped working. Knowing what something does well is only half the story. Once you understand where it stops being the best answer, you understand it more completely and can better maximize its potential.
I’ve found AI is no different.
Tools aren’t inherently good or bad. They’re often simply better or worse at solving particular problems. The challenge isn’t deciding whether AI is the answer. The challenge is recognizing when it is…and when it isn’t.
Looking back, beyond some incremental skills, those projects weren’t teaching me something new overall. They were reminding me of something I’d momentarily lost in the frenzy: how to ask better questions.
The projects changed. The models changed. Even my opinions changed. The reminder stayed consistent. AI is a remarkable tool, and like every remarkable tool, it’s easy to become fascinated by the tool itself. I’ve certainly done that. It may even help explain why we’re seeing such different headlines around AI.
Last year, MIT reported that most AI initiatives hadn’t produced a positive ROI, largely attributing it to a knowledge gap. Around the same time, UC Berkeley argued that ROI was the wrong measure altogether and instead, that many organizations were creating value in ways traditional ROI calculations weren’t capturing. I think there’s truth in both perspectives, but before we debate how to measure success, we should probably agree on what success is supposed to look like in the first place.
Purpose should come before measurement.
The projects that have mattered most in my journey so far were the ones that reminded me to stop asking, “How can I use AI?” and resume asking, “What problem am I actually trying to solve?”
Everything else tends to follow from there.
